title: Non-linear density scaling of spin noise reveals atomic correlations in warm vapors
authors: Joseph Delpy, Elwyn Cardoz, Adwaith KV, Nikos Fayard, Nadia Belabas, Fabien bretenaker, Fabienne Goldfarb
abstract: We experimentally demonstrate a non-linear dependence of the spin noise variance on atomic density in a warm alkali vapor. Implementing high-bandwidth spin noise spectroscopy (SNS) near the D2 transition of rubidium, a quadratic spin noise contribution is shown to arise at high densities, in contrast with the linear dependence valid in non-interacting ensembles. This non-linear scaling is shown to crucially depend on the residual optical excitation of the vapor by the probe beam, suggesting it stems from atomic cross-correlations due to resonant dipole-dipole interaction (DDI) in the vapor. We support this claim by introducing an additional experimental protocol to quench the ddi, resulting in a suppression of both the quadratic scaling of the spin variance and the distortions of the spin noise spectrum induced by the interaction. These results extend the applications of SNS to the characterization of many-body correlations in complex quantum systems.
summary: The researchers used high-bandwidth spin noise spectroscopy on warm rubidium vapor to measure how spin noise changes with atomic density. They found a non-linear, quadratic increase in noise at high densities, which they attribute to resonant dipole-dipole interactions between atoms. By experimentally quenching these interactions, they confirmed that this non-linear scaling is indeed a signature of atomic cross-correlations.
main_problem: To experimentally characterize non-linear atomic correlations in warm alkali vapors by measuring the density dependence of spin noise.
main_result: The spin noise variance exhibits a quadratic, superlinear dependence on atomic density at high concentrations, which is attributed to resonant dipole-dipole interactions (DDI).
method: High-bandwidth Spin Noise Spectroscopy (SNS) is employed to measure Faraday rotation fluctuations in a warm rubidium vapor.
model_or_system: The system is a warm alkali vapor (rubidium) probed by a detuned laser, where the noise measurement probes spin correlations arising from resonant dipole-dipole interactions (DDI).
key_observables: Spin noise variance ($\langle \delta	heta^2 angle$) and the spin noise power spectral density (PSD).
important_parameters: Vapor density ($n$), detuning ($\Delta$), and input probe power ($P_p$).
main_assumptions: The non-linear scaling is assumed to arise from DDI, which is tested by quenching the interaction using an auxiliary resonant beam.
figures_summary: Figures show the non-linear (quadratic) scaling of spin noise variance vs. density, and demonstrate the suppression of this non-linearity and spectral distortions when the DDI is quenched.
paper_structure: The paper establishes the linear regime (non-interacting) and then presents evidence for a superlinear, quadratic scaling at high densities. The core argument is built by introducing and then experimentally suppressing the DDI via an auxiliary beam, confirming the correlation origin.
why_interesting: This work provides a direct, measurable spectroscopic signature (non-linear noise scaling) for many-body quantum correlations in a simple, controllable atomic system, extending SNS beyond simple magnetization measurements.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 2. Experimental spin noise spectra. Examples of spin noise PSD obtained for densities below and above 1014 at.cm−3, for a detuning ∆/2π = +1.5 GHz and a 2 mW input probe power.

Figure 3. Evolution of the absolute spin noise vari- ance with vapor density. (a) Evolution of the integrated spin noise variance as a function of atom density for a de- tuning ∆/2π = +1.5 GHz, a 2 mW probe power and 0.4 mW detected power. Red dashed line: linear fit to the data up to n = 1014 at.cm−3. Color dashed lines indicate the cor- responding spin noise PSD in Fig. 2. (b) Residuals from the linear regression, fitted with a quadratic function for n > 1.5 × 1014 at.cm−3 (red dashed line). Inset: close-up view of the low-density data points.

Figure 4. Dependence of the spin noise scaling on the probe detuning. Evolution of the spin noise vari- ance as a function of vapor density for a detuning of (a) ∆/2π = +4.5 GHz and (b) ∆/2π = −7 GHz (green squares), ∆/2π = 8 GHz (orange dots). Dashed and dash-dotted lines are linear fit to the data for n < 1014 at.cm−3. Insets: resid- uals from linear fits from respective color-coded data.

Figure 5. Evolution of the measured spin noise on the input probe power. Detected spin noise variance as a function of input probe power for different vapor densities. The detected power is fixed to 160 µW for all points, and the detuning is ∆/2π = +1.5 GHz.

Figure 6. Impact of the auxiliary beam on the de- tected spin noise variance. (a) Density dependence of the residuals from the low-density linear trend with (blue dots) and without (orange squares) auxiliary beam. Param- eters are Pp = 2 mW, Pd = 0.4 mW and ∆/2π = +1.5 GHz. (b) Blue dots: evolution of the residuals with auxiliary beam power at a fixed density of 3.3 × 1014 at.cm−3. Orange tri- angles: transmission of the auxiliary beam, fitted with a saturated optical depth function (red dash-dotted line).
